summary: DeVault writes a satirical obituary for Mozilla, arguing that the company he once admired effectively died around 2011 when it began making compromises. He recounts his personal history with Firefox from high school through its competition with Chrome, praising its speed, customizability, and open-source ethos. He argues that the post-2011 Mozilla is a fundamentally different organization that betrays the original's values through mandatory non-free add-ons, locked-down extension APIs, and misguided projects like Firefox OS. The piece frames modern Mozilla as an impostor wearing the skin of a once-great champion of the open web.
key_points: Firefox was once the quintessential hacker's browser — fast, customizable, open source, and a credible steward of the open internet
Mozilla's decline began with the rapid-release version numbering change in 2011, which DeVault frames as the first in a series of compromises
Modern Firefox ships with unremovable non-free add-ons, a cryptographic walled garden for extensions, and bloated features like video conferencing
Mozilla wasted resources on doomed projects like Firefox OS, running HTML/CSS/JS on the weakest hardware with the slowest rendering engine
The organization that now calls itself Mozilla is fundamentally different from and inferior to the one that earned the community's trust
money_quotes: I remember a time when I would tell my friends that I'd gladly appoint Mozilla as the steward of the open internet over the W3C.
Firefox truly became a hacker's web browser.
They made the first of many compromises when they switched to bumping the major version with each release. Mozilla died in April of 2011.
This new company had good intentions, but has completely lost the spirit of Mozilla.

This new browser is a stain on Mozilla's legacy - it ships with unremovable nonfree add-ons, removes huge swaths of the original add-on API, includes a cryptographically walled garden for add-ons, and apparently now includes an instant messaging and video conferencing platform.

They tried to make a mobile phone OS whose UI was powered by technology that's been proven to produce an inferior mobile experience (HTML+CSS+JS) using the slowest rendering engine on the market (gecko) on the lowest powered phones on the market.
To the new company that calls itself Mozilla: you do an injustice to the memory of Mozilla.
key_insight: Mozilla betrayed its founding mission as champion of the open web by shipping non-free components, locking down extensibility, and chasing failed side projects, becoming the very kind of organization it was created to oppose.
